images.alt:
A vertical, high-contrast digital painting executed in a heavy impasto oil style, dominated by shades of slate, silver, and charcoal grey. The texture of the background is thick and tactile, with visible palette knife strokes and ridges of paint creating a weathered, stone-like wall. Centered in the composition is a slender, dark silhouette of a feminine figure, her body rendered with rough, vertical brushstrokes that give her an elongated, ethereal appearance. She is walking upward or forward into the composition, her back to the viewer. Her right arm is raised high above her head, supporting a delicate, spindly set of scales. The scales are slightly tilted, with small, dark weights in each pan, suggesting a precarious balance. The lighting appears to come from the upper right, casting a long, soft shadow of the figure onto the textured floor below. The overall mood is somber, reflective, and starkly minimalist.

Keywords:
Justice, scales, silhouette, impasto, monochrome, solitude, balance, texture, symbolic, fine art, grey, ethereal.

record.text:
Whoa 🤯

The Moon, in full eclipse, with the #Artemis II Orion spacecraft. Part of the Moon and spacecraft are lit by Earthshine, and both Saturn and Mars are visible to the lower right. Incredible. Details: images.nasa.gov/details/art0...

images.alt: [alt text by NASA] The Moon, seen here backlit by the Sun during a solar eclipse on April 6, 2026, is photographed by one of the cameras on the Orion spacecraft’s solar array wings. Orion is visible in the foreground on the left. Earth is reflecting sunlight at the left edge of the Moon, which is slightly brighter than the rest of the disk. The bright spot visible just below the Moon’s bottom right edge is Saturn. Beyond that, the bright spot at the right edge of the image is Mars. Credit: NASA

[alt text by NASA] The Moon, seen here backlit by the Sun during a solar eclipse on April 6, 2026, is photographed by one of the cameras on the Orion spacecraft’s solar array wings. Orion is visible in the foreground on the left. Earth is reflecting sunlight at the left edge of the Moon, which is slightly brighter than the rest of the disk. The bright spot visible just below the Moon’s bottom right edge is Saturn. Beyond that, the bright spot at the right edge of the image is Mars. Credit: NASA
